content:
Before now, I always used Flash and Reverse-Flash to solve anything LinkedList. Two speedsters racing down central city in my head, Flash always faster than Reverse-Flash, somehow always meeting at the finish line. It worked. Until today.
Today I sat with the Intersection of Two Linked Lists problem and Flash just... refused to show up. My brain kept asking, "but why would he be faster if they’re literally just nodes moving one at a time?" and I had no answer. The metaphor cracked.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

image from PinterestSo I let it go and reached for something closer to home instead, Lagos standstill traffic. You know the one, five lanes of chaos on the mainland, everybody trying to cross over to the faster lane, and then somehow, as you approach the bridge, the lanes just start disappearing. Five becomes three. Three becomes two. Doesn’t matter which lane you started in, Ojota or Anthony, you all end up merging onto the same strip of road before Third Mainland Bridge.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

image from PinterestThat was it. That was the whole problem. Two different lists (two different lanes), different lengths before the merge, but once you hit the merge point, it’s the exact same road all the way to the end. No speed required. No racing. Just finish your lane, if you run out of road hop into the other person’s lane and keep moving, and somehow, beautifully, you both arrive at the merge point on the same step.
```
function getIntersectionNode(headA: ListNode | null, headB: ListNode | null): ListNode | null {
let carOne = headA
let carTwo = headB
while (carOne !== carTwo) {
carOne = carOne === null ? headB :
carOne.next
carTwo = carTwo === null ? headA : carTwo.next
}
return carOne
};
```
here’s proof it works
Press enter or click to view image in full size

image from PinterestTurns out I didn’t need superheroes. I needed Lagos traffic.
return 💗;
